docs.text: Introduction Hello basement dwellers, and welcome to the documentation for Douglang, the programming language of Doug's basement. If you're caught writing anything else, you'll be arrested (This does not include contributing to Douglang or reimplementing old smelly code in Douglang). Douglang relies on TTS, and after a grueling one hour of searching, our scientists found the voice that Doug uses for TTS. Although it (should) run on most computers, it is intended for Windows as the TTS voice is specifically a Microsoft SAPI5 voice. Important : The voice will only work on Windows. If you are on Windows, you can get the voice from harposoftware.com , which still sells the voices. They also provide a 30 day free trial! Even More Importanter : When going through the voice's installer, make sure you download the program called "Speech2Go". This program contains DLLs that are required for Geraint to work. If it doesn't prompt you to download it, after the installer check if you have it regardless, as it may have just done it for you! However, it's listed as costing "euros." We've never heard of that currency, but it's definitely not channel points, so we can't gamba for it. They do provide a 30 day free trial, but as this language was created within that 30 day period, nobody knows what happens to the voice after the 30 days. Some believe if you use it past the free trial, your execution is scheduled for next Tuesday. If you can't get the voice, then it will default to whatever voice it can find. That isn't Twitch Chat, but I guess it will suffice. In the future, using Amazon Polly might be an option. Until then, only hot and rich individuals can use the language to its fullest potential.
docs.title: Introduction

docs.title: Introduction

docs.text: Control Flow Douglang doesn't have much control flow. Our scientists are still figuring out what "structured programming" even means. That means no functions or fancy loops. I'm sure we'll get them eventually, it's only a matter of time. But let's focus on the present, because the present is a gift, that's why it's called the present. To loop, say loop . Confusing, I know. Don't worry, I believe in you. loop [ tts "I'm going to say this forever." ] Loops go forever. Why do they go forever? Because if they didn't loop, they wouldn't be a loop . You can end a loop with break : loop [ tts "What is my purpose? Just to loop? Forever?" break // Oh, thank you. ] Except now it doesn't loop and therefore doesn't deserve to be called loop . We can fix this with predictions. prediction (Doug) = "Douglas Wreden" [ Believers win [ tts "Hey Doug" ] ] And, what's that? = is for assignment? No, This is Douglang. We use set for assignment. That frees up = for conditionals, instead of the disgusting and vulgar == . The Believers win block runs whenever the condition is true, because the Believers believe it will evaluate to true, and they won. The same would happen with a Doubters win block, because the Doubters believe it will evaluate to false. Combine that with loop and now you have a loop that doesn't go on forever. I don't know why you would want that, because loops going forever is cool. // Laundry alarm Doug set 5 loop [ Bald set "Laundry in " +set (Doug) +set "." tts Doug -set 1 prediction (Doug) = 0 [ Believers win [ break ] ] ] tts "LAUNDRY!!!!!!!"
docs.title: Control Flow

Confusing, I know. Don't worry, I believe in you. loop [ tts "I'm going to say this forever." ] Loops go forever. Why do they go forever? Because if they didn't loop, they wouldn't be a loop . You can end a loop with break : loop [ tts "What is my purpose? Just to loop? Forever?" break // Oh, thank you. ] Except now it doesn't loop and therefore doesn't deserve to be called loop . We can fix this with predictions. prediction (Doug) = "Douglas Wreden" [ Believers win [ tts "Hey Doug" ] ] And, what's that? = is for assignment? No, This is Douglang. We use set for assignment. That frees up = for conditionals, instead of the disgusting and vulgar == . The Believers win block runs whenever the condition is true, because the Believers believe it will evaluate to true, and they won. The same would happen with a Doubters win block, because the Doubters believe it will evaluate to false. Combine that with loop and now you have a loop that doesn't go on forever. docs.title: Control Flow

docs.text: Hey Doug Hello World in Douglang is easy. Douglang doesn't support printing, instead, it has TTS similar to what happens when Doug steals your money takes a donation during a livestream. Simply write tts , then a string. tts "Hey Doug" The tts keyword reads it out. Significantly better than printing.
docs.title: Hey Doug

docs.title: Hey Doug

docs.text: Variables Here at Basement Technologies Inc , we think variables are for the weak. As such, Douglang doesn't have them. In their place, we write the name of our captor benefactor, Douglas Scott Wreden. Doug set "goot" Let's explain that code block. At first glance, it might look like variables. Trust me, it's not. What this is actually doing is indexing a signed array and setting its value to "goot". Yes, you read that right: a signed array. Our scientists were hard at work finding a solution to the modern problem of variables, and this is what they made. So, what the hell even is a signed array? Well, you know how arrays are indexed like my_array[i] ? Well, we thought that it's kinda stupid for indices to only be positive (unsigned). So we poured all of our channel points into developing a new array with negative indices. That means array[-10] is correct. However, Douglang doesn't use that old , smelly , decrepit syntax. Instead, you use Doug Notation ! Cue applause. Doug Notation is really quite simple. You write Doug . That's it. So: Doug What index is that? It's obviously 1. Because there's only one Doug. Doug Notation is similar to Brainfuck's < and > . Don't worry, it's very simple. It only has three rules. Chained Dougs: Jump count, in powers of two. Doug is 1, DougDoug is 2, DougDougDoug is 4, DougDougDougDoug is 8, and so on. Sequences: Sequences of Doug Chains combine with each other. They are separated by whitespace. Even and Odd Chains: Sequences count the number of chains they have. The sequence DougDoug Doug has two chains, for instance. And in that sequence, DougDoug is an odd chain as it is the 1st chain in the sequence, and Doug is an even chain as it is the second chain. Odd chains: Perform addition. Even chains: Perform subtraction. So, DougDoug Doug performs + 2 - 1 on the index. Or, more simply, is equivalent to just writing Doug . Simple, right? With that in mind, getting to 11: DougDougDougDougDoug // 16 DougDougDoug // -4. Now at 12 Doug // 1. Now at 13 DougDoug // -2. Now at 11 And if you want to reset the index back to 0: Bald . Simple, right? That's how you access variables. But how do you use them? set "goot" The set keyword sets the value at the current index. Paired with Doug Notation: Bald set "goot" That sets the 0th index's value to "goot" . Because set overrides the previous value, there are other set keywords that simply change it. They are: +set -set *set /set %set Note, these do not work like + , - , etc in other languages. They work exactly like set , except instead of overwriting the value stored at the index, they perform an operation on it. And, that's everything about variables. Or, actually, I'm getting word from the technicians at Basement Technologies Inc that there's actually one thing left: Doug Expressions. Doug Expressions look exactly like regular Doug Notation, with two differences: They're in parenthesis They are always relative to index 0 They don't move the index And evaluate to the value stored at that index And yes, that's definitely two differences. Don't let the numbers fool you. So, if you were to store something in index 2, you can say either DougDoug tts to move the index to 2 and tts, but that's unreliable if the index isn't already close to 2, or you want to keep the index pointer where it is. So, instead, you can say tts (DougDoug) which always evaluates to whatever is stored at DougDoug . So, you've been taught enough about variables. You know enough to understand what this does! set "Hey, Doug. " Doug set 42 Doug set "The answer to life, the universe, and everything is " Bald +set (DougDoug) +set (Doug) +set "." tts Also, make sure you count properly. Douglang has an irrational fear of people not knowing how to count. This means before you store something at index 4, something has to be at index 3, and so on. Same for negative indices. So, no DougDougDoug before you have something at DougDoug Doug .
docs.title: Variables
